text.title: Client-Conventions Reverse-Engineering Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules. Each rule prevents a specific failure mode in AI-generated client code: cargo-culting, hallucinated rules, drift, missing the structural rules.

rule.statement: The first pass MUST exhaustively dump the active lint/format rules: every config file in the repo (.eslintrc, pyproject.toml, .editorconfig, .prettierrc, Makefile lint targets, pre-commit hooks). The guardrail file MUST list these as "machine-enforced rules — do not duplicate," then proceed to extract the unwritten conventions on top.
rule.rationale: Reverse-engineering rules the linter already enforces wastes context budget and risks contradicting the linter. The linter wins on conflicts, so the guardrail must be linter-aware.

rule.statement: Every convention claim in the guardrail file MUST be backed by >= 2 concrete examples from existing code (file paths) OR >= 2 review comments from merged PRs. Inferring "they probably want camelCase" with no evidence is forbidden. If only one example exists, mark the rule "tentative" and re-verify on first PR.

rationale.source: Pattern-mining research; cf. style-rule discovery in tools like NaturalCC

rule.rationale: Single-example or vibe-based rules cause an agent to hallucinate plausible but wrong conventions. Two-example floor catches most accidental over-specifications.

rule.statement: Conventions MUST cover at minimum: file/directory layering (where does X go?), naming (file, class, function, test name pattern), import discipline (relative vs absolute, allowed external imports), test placement (co-located vs separate dir), and architecture boundaries (which layer may call which). A guardrail file that covers only formatting is incomplete and will produce structurally-wrong code.

rationale.source: Field experience with AI agents on enterprise codebases

rule.rationale: Formatting rules are the visible 10%. Structural and layering rules are the 90% that determine whether the PR fits. Missing them produces functionally-correct, architecturally-wrong code.

rule.statement: The guardrail MUST capture dependency policy: which package manager, version-pinning style (exact / caret / range), allowed vs disallowed registries, security review process for new deps, and whether agent is allowed to add deps without explicit approval. Default if unclear: agent is NOT allowed to add deps without flagging.
rule.rationale: Agents adding deps without policy awareness create supply-chain incidents — accidentally adopting unmaintained or vulnerable packages. Default-deny is the only safe stance.

rule.statement: The guardrail file MUST include a "review feedback append" section. When a client reviewer rejects a PR for a convention reason not captured, the developer appends the new rule with evidence and re-feeds it to the agent. The guardrail file is a living artifact, not a one-time snapshot.

rationale.source: Continuous-improvement / kaizen practice; cf. retrospective discipline

rule.rationale: The first guardrail draft never captures everything. Without an explicit write-back loop, the same convention violation recurs PR after PR and the agent stays uncalibrated.

example.title: Evidence-backed convention rule
example.code: Rule (structural): Repository pattern — controllers MUST NOT call ORM directly. Use the repository layer in src/repos/. Evidence: - src/controllers/order_controller.py:42 (uses OrderRepo) - src/controllers/user_controller.py:18 (uses UserRepo) - PR #1247 review comment: "Move ORM call to UserRepo; we keep ORM out of controllers." Verified-by: human, 2026-05-20
example.note: Three citations, clear instruction the agent can act on, verification date.

example.title: Unverified vibe rule
example.code: Rule: "Use TypeScript strict mode everywhere." Evidence: none provided.
example.note: Probably right, possibly wrong. tsconfig actually has noImplicitAny: false in three sub-projects. The agent will toggle strict and break a build.
